message.content:
You are auditing ONE documentation file in a ROS2 robotics project (TurtleBot 4 autonomous "guide robot").
Read these files: /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/ros2-over-nuwave.md (the target), and /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md (project context). If the doc makes claims about code/launch/config, spot-check against the real files under /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/src/.
Topic hint: ROS2 over NUwave.

ACTUAL CODE/CONFIG ON DISK (ground truth as of this audit):
ROS2 package src/guide_mate_explorer/:
  nodes: bfs_explorer.py, glass_guard.py, depth_lidar_fusion.py, combined_node.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  launch: autonomous_mapping.launch.py, depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py, depth_perception.launch.py, explore.launch.py, combined.launch.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  config: nav2_glass.yaml, oakd_mapping.yaml, slam_fused.yaml(UNTRACKED-ish/new)
NEW untracked C++ package src/guide_mate_perception/:
  src/depth_lidar_fusion_node.cpp, CMakeLists.txt, package.xml, launch/depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py
rviz/: launch_rviz.sh, turtlebot468_mapping.rviz (tracked)
Root: README.md, CLAUDE.md, .gitignore (tracked); start.sh (gitignored)
Vendored third-party clone (gitignored, untracked, on-disk only): aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ (full AWS SDK repo with its own .git, ~20 markdowns)
On-disk credentials (gitignored, never committed): Turtlebot-468.cert.pem, Turtlebot-468.private.key, Turtlebot-468.public.key, Turtlebot-468-Policy, root-CA.crt, c9109663-*AccessToken.txt, connect_device_package.zip

Assess the target doc honestly and critically. importance: 'critical'=needed to operate/understand the robot or repo; 'useful'=helpful reference; 'marginal'=nice-to-have/thin; 'obsolete'=stale/superseded/empty-stub. Many tiny section-index README.md files may be 'marginal' boilerplate. Flag any factual drift from the actual code inventory. Be specific in uniqueValue about what is irreplaceably documented here (real hardware findings, IPs, gotchas) vs. generic restatement.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll audit the target documentation file. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
1	# ROS 2 over NUwave — What Works and What Doesn't
2	
3	Compatibility findings for running ROS 2 (Humble, FastDDS) between a **lab laptop** and a **TurtleBot 4** when both are on **NUwave**. Verified June 2026 with `turtlebot468`.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Verdict: ✅ It works (laptop ↔ robot, full ROS 2)
8	After two fixes (below), a laptop on NUwave can discover, subscribe, publish, and call services on the robot across the network.
9	
10	| Capability | Status |
11	|---|---|
12	| Robot on NUwave, ROS stack healthy | ✅ |
13	| SSH to robot, run ROS 2 on robot | ✅ |
14	| Topic **discovery** from laptop | ✅ full `/turtlebot468/*` graph |
15	| **Subscribe** (battery / odom / scan) | ✅ ~1 Hz / ~17.8 Hz / ~7.6 Hz |
16	| **Publish** laptop → robot (cmd_audio beep) | ✅ |
17	| **Service / param** calls | ✅ (`wifi.interface → wlan0`) |
18	
19	> Motion actions (undock, drive) were intentionally **not** exercised in this verification pass.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## The working laptop setup (use this)
24	ROS 2 default **multicast** discovery does **not** work here (laptop and robot land on different `/19` subnets; multicast is link-local). The robot runs a **FastDDS Discovery Server** (unicast, routable). Configure the laptop as a **super-client via environment variables** — *not* via an XML profile (the XML profile silently fails to apply, see gotchas):
25	
26	```bash
27	source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
28	export RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_fastrtps_cpp
29	export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=0
30	export ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER="10.247.204.21:11811"   # robot Pi IP : 11811 (DHCP — verify on robot display)
31	export ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True
32	unset FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE                 # ensure no stale XML interferes
33	```
34	Then:
35	```bash
36	ros2 daemon stop && ros2 daemon start && sleep 10    # keep ONE daemon with this env
37	ros2 topic list | grep -c turtlebot468               # ~28-30 once the stack is up
38	ros2 topic echo /turtlebot468/battery_state --once   # real data within ~20 s
39	```
40	Notes:
41	- The robot IP is **DHCP** — if `10.247.204.21` stops responding, read the current IP off the robot's display.
42	- Every fresh CLI context pays ~10 s of discovery. Keep one daemon running with the env above.
43	- `ros2 topic echo/list/info` support `--no-daemon`; **`ros2 topic hz` does not**.
44	
45	---
46	
47	## Network facts (measured)
48	| | IP | Subnet |
49	|---|---|---|
50	| Robot (`turtlebot468`) Pi | `10.247.204.21/19` | `10.247.192.0/19` |
51	| Laptop | `10.247.228.155/19` | `10.247.224.0/19` |
52	| Gateway | `10.247.224.1` | routes between them |
53	
54	- **Different `/19` segments**, routed. ICMP, TCP (SSH), and **UDP both directions** all pass between them.
55	- **NUwave is NOT doing harmful client isolation** — a raw UDP beacon robot→laptop was received cleanly, and DDS unicast works once configured correctly. (An early "0 / 40 packets" result was a **transient**, not a firewall; a later beacon got 13/13 with no firewall changes.)
56	- **Multicast** can't cross the two subnets → Discovery Server (unicast) is the only viable model. The robots are already set up this way.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Root causes found & fixed (the debugging trail)
61	Four separate problems, each masked the next:
62	
63	1. **Robot couldn't see NUwave at all.** WiFi regulatory domain was unset (`regdom=00`), which disables 5 GHz; the strong nearby NUwave APs are 5 GHz. → Set country to **US**. See [nuwave-connection.md](nuwave-connection.md).
64	
65	2. **All Create 3 base topics silent** (`battery_state`, `odom`, `dock_status`, every motion command/action) — found robot-side during the compatibility check, *unrelated to NUwave*. The Create 3 was configured to register with a discovery server at **`192.168.50.31:11811`** — a stale IP from the previous lab network, unreachable over its wired link. → Fixed via the base's web config at `http://192.168.186.2/ros-config`: set the discovery server to **`192.168.186.3:11811`** (the Pi's usb0 address), then `curl -X POST http://192.168.186.2/api/restart-app`. Base topics returned within ~2 min.
66	
67	3. **Discovery worked but zero data — attempt 1.** The laptop super-client was configured via an **XML profile (`FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE`)** that **silently did not apply** (behaved as a plain client → no full graph). → Use the **`ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True` env var** instead. This restored topic discovery (all 28 topics).
68	
69	4. **Discovery worked, still zero data — the real bug.** The robot's FastDDS **discovery-server database was corrupted**: it received the laptop's endpoint announcements but **failed to relay them** to the robot's own nodes (confirmed robot-side via tcpdump + `DISCOVERY_DATABASE` error logs; the robot reported `Subscription count: 0` for the laptop's reader, and a new laptop-published topic never appeared in the robot's own graph). → **Restart the discovery server + the full robot ROS stack** (`sudo systemctl restart discovery && sleep 3 && sudo systemctl restart turtlebot4`). After restart, data flowed immediately.
70	
71	Health check for #4 (run on the robot): `sudo journalctl -u discovery | grep -c "DISCOVERY_DATABASE Error"` — non-zero and growing means restart the discovery service.
72	
73	### How #4 presented (so you recognize it again)
74	- `ros2 topic list` from the laptop shows the full graph. ✅
75	- `ros2 topic echo <topic>` hangs forever, no data. ❌
76	- On the robot: `ros2 topic info -v <topic>` shows `Subscription count: 0` even while the laptop subscriber is live.
77	- A topic *published* from the laptop does **not** appear in the robot's own `ros2 topic list`.
78	- **Fix:** restart the discovery service and robot nodes (interrupts robot ROS ~30 s).
79	
80	---
81	
82	## Other findings
83	- **Clocks: fine once booted and online.** Robot NTP-synced (chrony → `pool.ntp.org`, ~µs off); laptop NTP-synced. Timestamps/TF consistent. (Robot timezone is cosmetically `America/Los_Angeles` — does not affect ROS, which uses UTC epoch.) **Caveat:** the Pi has no RTC and boots with a stale clock until WiFi connects — see [network README](README.md#time-sync).
84	- **Robot-side scripting gotcha:** `/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash` force-sets `ROS_SUPER_CLIENT` from a TTY check (`[ -t 0 ]`), so non-interactive shells get `False` and see an empty graph. In scripts on the robot, `export ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True` **after** sourcing it.
85	- **Bandwidth caution:** camera `image_raw` topics can saturate WiFi — test last, with `--once`, and prefer `compressed` variants.
86	- **OAK-D camera (robot 468):** throws recurring `X_LINK_ERROR` communication failures — a hardware/USB issue, unrelated to networking; camera topics exist but the device is unhealthy.
87	- **Create 3:** stays on the wired USB link (Discovery Server), correctly never on NUwave.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## Operational checklist
92	- [ ] Robot Pi on NUwave (5 GHz enabled, `regdom=US`).
93	- [ ] Laptop on NUwave; env vars set as above (`ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True`, `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER=<robot-ip>:11811`).
94	- [ ] One `ros2 daemon` running with that env.
95	- [ ] If discovery works but data doesn't → restart the robot's discovery server + stack (root cause #4).
96	- [ ] If base topics (battery/odom/cmd_vel) are silent while Pi topics work → check the Create 3's discovery-server address (root cause #2).
97	- [ ] Robot IP is DHCP — confirm current IP on the robot display if it changes.
98	
99	## Reproduce the key tests
100	```bash
101	# data flow (the critical one)
102	ros2 topic echo /turtlebot468/battery_state --once
103	# sustained rates (hz needs the daemon, NOT --no-daemon)
104	ros2 topic hz /turtlebot468/odom        # ~18 Hz
105	ros2 topic hz /turtlebot468/scan        # ~7-8 Hz
106	# publish laptop -> robot (audible beep)
107	ros2 topic pub --once /turtlebot468/cmd_audio irobot_create_msgs/msg/AudioNoteVector \
108	  "{append: false, notes: [{frequency: 660, max_runtime: {sec: 0, nanosec: 500000000}}]}"
109	# service / param
110	ros2 param get /turtlebot468/turtlebot4_node wifi.interface     # -> wlan0
111	```
112

content.text: Let me read the sibling network docs and the project context to assess redundancy and cross-references.

content.content:
1	# Network Overview
2	
3	This section documents the network setup for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 robots.
4	
5	## Documents
6	- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](nuwave-connection.md)
7	- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](ros2-over-nuwave.md)
8	
9	## The two-computer architecture
10	A TurtleBot 4 is **two networked computers**:
11	
12	| Component | Role | WiFi capability |
13	|---|---|---|
14	| **Raspberry Pi 4** | Runs Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble. The host you SSH into. | 2.4 + 5 GHz (dual-band) |
15	| **iRobot Create 3 base** | Mobility base (wheels, IMU, bumpers, docking). | 2.4 GHz only, **WPA2-Personal only** |
16	
17	They are joined by the **USB-C cable**, which carries both power and a **wired Ethernet link** on the `192.168.186.0/24` subnet (Pi = `.3`, Create 3 = `.2`).
18	
19	### Why the Create 3 never joins NUwave
20	The Create 3's firmware does **not** support WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / PEAP) — it can only join a network with a single shared password. NUwave is enterprise, so the Create 3 **cannot** join it. This is fine because the robots use **Discovery Server** mode: the Create 3 keeps WiFi disabled and routes everything through the Pi over the USB-C link. Only the **Pi** connects to NUwave.
21	
22	## Discovery model: FastDDS Discovery Server
23	ROS 2's default discovery is **multicast**, which is link-local and **will not cross IP subnets**. On NUwave the Pi and a laptop typically land on **different `/19` segments**, so multicast discovery is a non-starter.
24	
25	The robots instead run a **FastDDS Discovery Server** on the Pi (`fast-discovery-server -i 0 -p 11811`, listening on `0.0.0.0:11811`). This uses **unicast**, which routes across subnets. Clients (the Pi's own ROS nodes, and any laptop) point at the server via `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`.
26	
27	Relevant environment on the Pi (`/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash`):
28	```
29	ROS_DOMAIN_ID=0
30	RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_fastrtps_cpp
31	ROS_LOCALHOST_ONLY=0
32	ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER=127.0.0.1:11811;
33	FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE=/etc/turtlebot4/fastdds_rpi.xml
34	```
35	> **Cleaned up 2026-06-12:** stale `192.168.50.x` entries from a previous network were
36	> removed from three places on robot 468: the Pi's `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`
37	> (`192.168.50.31:11811`), the **Create 3's own discovery-server address** (same stale
38	> IP — this one was *not* harmless: it silenced every base topic; see
39	> [ros2-over-nuwave.md](ros2-over-nuwave.md)), and chrony's preferred NTP server
40	> (`192.168.50.146` → `pool.ntp.org`, verified working over NUwave). After any future
41	> network change, check for relics: `grep -r "192.168.50" /etc/`
42	> Robot **436 has not been migrated or cleaned up yet** — same steps apply.
43	
44	## Time sync
45	Chain: internet NTP (`pool.ntp.org`, with `ntp.ubuntu.com` backup) → Pi (chrony) →
46	Create 3 (chrony serves `192.168.186.0/24`). **Gotcha:** the Pi has **no RTC** — it
47	boots with a stale clock (whatever it was at last shutdown) until chrony steps it once
48	WiFi connects. Services started before the step see a huge time jump; check
49	`chronyc tracking` when debugging anything time-related.
50	
51	## Addressing summary (as configured June 2026)
52	| Host | NUwave IP (example) | Notes |
53	|---|---|---|
54	| `turtlebot468` Pi | `10.247.204.21/19` | DHCP — will change |
55	| Create 3 (468) | `192.168.186.2` | wired-only, via Pi |
56	| Lab laptop | `10.247.228.155/19` | different `/19` than the Pi |
57	
58	> NUwave IPs are DHCP and **change** on reconnect. The robot shows its current IP on its display. On the lab `ASUS_98` network the Pis were previously `10.91.221.14` (468) and `10.91.221.95` (436).
59

1	# Connecting a TurtleBot 4 (Raspberry Pi) to NUwave
2	
3	How we got the TurtleBot 4's Raspberry Pi onto Northeastern's **NUwave** (WPA2-Enterprise, PEAP/MSCHAPv2) network — including the non-obvious blocker that cost us the most time.
4	
5	> **Scope:** this connects the **Raspberry Pi** only. The Create 3 base cannot and does not need to join NUwave (see [network overview](README.md)).
6	
7	> ⚠️ **Credentials:** never commit real NUwave credentials to this repo. Placeholders below: `<nuwave-username>` (e.g. `pandya.kal`) and `<nuwave-password>`.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## TL;DR
12	1. The real blocker was **not** credentials — the Pi's WiFi **regulatory domain was unset (`00`)**, which **disables the 5 GHz band**. NUwave here is on 5 GHz, so the robot literally couldn't *see* the network. Your phone could, the robot couldn't.
13	2. Fix: set the WiFi country to **US** → 5 GHz enabled → NUwave becomes visible.
14	3. Then add a NUwave NetworkManager profile (PEAP/MSCHAPv2) and connect.
15	4. Keep `ASUS_98` as an automatic fallback so a failed attempt can't strand the robot.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Background: the symptom
20	- The robot could only ever see `ASUS_98` (the local 2.4 GHz lab router) in its WiFi scan.
21	- NUwave, eduroam, and **every** other AP were invisible to the robot — even after forced rescans.
22	- A phone standing next to the robot saw NUwave fine.
23	
24	## Root cause: 5 GHz disabled by an unset regulatory domain
25	On a Raspberry Pi, if the WiFi **country code is not set**, the kernel uses the world regulatory domain (`00`), which **disables 5 GHz channels**. The robot was therefore effectively a 2.4 GHz-only radio. Diagnostic that confirmed it:
26	
27	```bash
28	cat /sys/module/cfg80211/parameters/ieee80211_regdom   # printed: 00
29	nmcli -t -f FREQ device wifi list                       # only 2437 MHz (2.4 GHz) ever appeared
30	```
31	A dual-band-capable Pi 4 seeing **zero** 5 GHz APs in a building full of them is the tell.
32	
33	## Fix step 1 — enable 5 GHz (set country = US)
34	```bash
35	# Make it permanent (survives reboots)
36	echo "options cfg80211 ieee80211_regdom=US" | sudo tee /etc/modprobe.d/cfg80211.conf
37	
38	# Apply now without rebooting (installs iw if missing)
39	command -v iw >/dev/null || sudo apt-get install -y iw
40	sudo iw reg set US
41	sleep 3
42	cat /sys/module/cfg80211/parameters/ieee80211_regdom    # must now print: US
43	```
44	If `apt-get` has no internet, instead `sudo reboot` — the modprobe file applies the country on boot, and the Pi auto-reconnects to `ASUS_98`.
45	
46	**Verify NUwave is now visible to the robot** (must succeed before continuing):
47	```bash
48	nmcli device wifi rescan; sleep 6
49	nmcli -f SSID,FREQ,SIGNAL,SECURITY device wifi list | grep -i nuwave
50	```
51	
52	## Fix step 2 — create the NUwave profile (autoconnect OFF for safe testing)
53	```bash
54	sudo nmcli connection add type wifi con-name NUwave ifname wlan0 ssid NUwave -- \
55	  wifi-sec.key-mgmt wpa-eap \
56	  802-1x.eap peap \
57	  802-1x.phase2-auth mschapv2 \
58	  802-1x.identity "<nuwave-username>" \
59	  802-1x.password "<nuwave-password>" \
60	  802-1x.domain-suffix-match "wireless.northeastern.edu" \
61	  802-1x.ca-cert /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt \
62	  connection.autoconnect no
63	```
64	Notes:
65	- **Domain:** NUwave's server certificate is validated against `wireless.northeastern.edu` (`domain-suffix-match`) using the system CA bundle. This is the correct, secure setup.
66	- **If** authentication fails on a cert error only, relax validation as a test:
67	  `sudo nmcli connection modify NUwave 802-1x.ca-cert "" 802-1x.domain-suffix-match ""` — try the secure version first.
68	- **Anonymous identity:** left blank, per NU's guidance.
69	
70	## Fix step 3 — test, with a 2-minute auto-revert safety net
71	⚠️ `wlan0` has one radio, so the instant NUwave associates the Pi **leaves `ASUS_98` and your SSH session drops** — expected on success *and* failure. The safety job forces a return to `ASUS_98` after 120 s no matter what, so you can't be locked out.
72	
73	```bash
74	# Arm the safety net first (survives your SSH session dying)
75	sudo bash -c 'nohup sh -c "sleep 120 && nmcli con up netplan-wlan0-ASUS_98" >/dev/null 2>&1 &'
76	
77	# Bring up NUwave
78	sudo nmcli connection up NUwave
79	```
80	Then **watch the robot's display** for its IP:
81	- **New `10.x` IP that persists** → connected. Re-SSH to the new IP.
82	- **IP returns to the `ASUS_98` address after ~2 min** → NUwave failed or was unusable; you're safely back on `ASUS_98`.
83	
84	## Fix step 4 — make it permanent (only after a successful test)
85	```bash
86	sudo nmcli connection modify NUwave connection.autoconnect yes connection.autoconnect-priority 10
87	```
88	`ASUS_98` (priority 0) remains the automatic fallback if NUwave ever drops.
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Safety model (why this can't brick the robot's networking)
93	- `ASUS_98` is a saved, auto-connecting profile and is the **only** auto-connecting WiFi → every boot and every failure returns there.
94	- The NUwave profile is created **autoconnect off** and tested manually first.
95	- The timed auto-revert guarantees recovery even if you lose the SSH session.
96	
97	## If you get locked out
98	A bad WiFi state can't be fixed over WiFi. Recovery requires physical access:
99	1. Plug a **monitor + USB keyboard** into the Pi (or an **Ethernet cable** into `eth0`).
100	2. Log in locally (`ubuntu` / robot password).
101	3. `sudo nmcli connection up netplan-wlan0-ASUS_98`
102	
103	---
104	
105	## Result (June 2026)
106	- `turtlebot468` Pi connected to NUwave at `10.247.204.21/19`.
107	- Robot's own ROS 2 stack fully healthy on NUwave (12 nodes, all topics).
108	- **Controlling the robot from a laptop over NUwave also works** — once the laptop is configured as a FastDDS super-client via env vars. See [ROS 2 over NUwave](ros2-over-nuwave.md) for the working recipe and the discovery-server gotchas we hit.
109

content.text: Now let me check the project context and spot-check a couple of the doc's technical claims against the actual code (the doc references namespacing, FastDDS, scan rates — let me verify the namespace and any cross-references are consistent).

content.content:
1	# cs7980 Guide-Mate — Documentation
2	
3	Working documentation for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 project.
4	
5	## Index
6	
7	### Network
8	- [Network overview](network/README.md) — how the robots are connected, addressing, discovery model
9	- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](network/nuwave-connection.md) — step-by-step, including the 5 GHz fix that was the real blocker
10	- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](network/ros2-over-nuwave.md) — compatibility findings
11	
12	### Camera
13	- [Camera overview](camera/README.md) — the OAK-D-LITE depth/RGB camera
14	- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](camera/oak-d-camera-test.md) — both streams work on USB 2; USB 3 boot-loop root-caused to power; bandwidth-limited frame drops
15	
16	### Mapping & navigation
17	- [Autonomous mapping — overview](mapping/README.md) — BFS frontier explorer + SLAM + Nav2, glass handling, how to run, status
18	- [Depth camera for mapping](mapping/depth-perception.md) — using the OAK-D depth to see glass the lidar can't: FOV, the height-filtered pipeline, compute, and the planned lidar-scan injection
19	- [Viewing live mapping in RViz](mapping/rviz-visualization.md) — laptop-side RViz for the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud; the tf-remap, QoS, and Lite-vs-Standard model gotchas
20	- Code: [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../src/guide_mate_explorer) — the `bfs_explorer` and `glass_guard` nodes
21	
22	### Power & battery
23	- [Power overview](power/README.md) — battery-powered robot; only `battery_state` metering; ~14 W idle
24	- [Power consumption & saving](power/power-saving.md) — measured draw, what's always running, and the working "park" (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed); what does *not* work
25	
26	### AWS IoT Core (cloud connectivity)
27	- [AWS IoT Core overview](aws-iot/README.md) — what we set up on robot 468, where it could help, and the security note on keeping credentials out of the repo
28	- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; how our files map to each
29	- [IoT Core ↔ other AWS services + EC2 access](aws-iot/service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core
30	- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](aws-iot/secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet
31	
32	---
33	
34	## ⚠️ Security note
35	**Do not commit credentials to this repo.** NUwave usernames/passwords, robot passwords, and any private keys must stay out of version control. The docs use placeholders such as `<nuwave-username>` and `<nuwave-password>`. Keep real values in a local, untracked file (e.g. `secrets.local`, already gitignored) or a password manager.
36	
37	## Quick facts
38	- **Robots:** two TurtleBot 4 units — `turtlebot468` (room 468) and `turtlebot436` (room 436).
39	- **Each robot = two computers:** a Raspberry Pi 4 (Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble — the thing you SSH into) and an iRobot Create 3 base (joined to the Pi by USB-C, which is both power and a wired Ethernet link).
40	- **Discovery model:** FastDDS **Discovery Server** running on the Pi; the Create 3 stays off WiFi and talks to the Pi over the USB-C link.
41	- **Default fallback network:** a local ASUS router, SSID `ASUS_98` (the robots auto-connect to it).
42

/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/README.md:20:The Create 3's firmware does **not** support WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / PEAP) — it can only join a network with a single shared password. /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/README.md:23:ROS 2's default discovery is **multicast**, which is link-local and **will not cross IP subnets**. On NUwave the Pi and a laptop typically land on **different `/19` segments**, so multicast discovery is a non-starter.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/README.md:25:The robots instead run a **FastDDS Discovery Server** on the Pi (`fast-discovery-server -i 0 -p 11811`, listening on `0.0.0.0:11811`). The real blocker was **not** credentials — the Pi's WiFi **regulatory domain was unset (`00`)**, which **disables the 5 GHz band**. NUwave here is on 5 GHz, so the robot literally couldn't *see* the network. Your phone could, the robot couldn't.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/nuwave-connection.md:13:2. Fix: set the WiFi country to **US** → 5 GHz enabled → NUwave becomes visible.

/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/mapping/README.md:41:| `depth_lidar_fusion` | The alternative to the separate `oakd/scan` source: **collapses** depth per column to the nearest non-floor point and **min-injects** it into the lidar scan → `scan_fused`. Point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` and the glass also lands in the **SLAM map**. Only lowers beams, never erases lidar; falls back to raw lidar if depth goes stale. Built + offline-validated. See [depth-perception.md](depth-perception.md). |
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/mapping/README.md:50:3. **`depth_lidar_fusion`** injects the depth scan into the lidar scan (`scan_fused`) so glass also lands in the **SLAM map**, not just the runtime costmap. *Built + offline-validated; on-robot localization check pending.*
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/mapping/README.md:92:- ⏭️ On robot: eyeball `scan` vs `scan_fused` in RViz → point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused` → localization sanity check → full mapping run.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:37:- `depth_lidar_fusion` — folds OAK-D depth into the lidar scan. Per-column vertical **collapse** (nearest non-floor pixel) → transform to the lidar frame via a one-time static TF (this accounts for the ~2 cm lidar↔camera offset) → **min-inject** into a copy of `/scan` → publishes `scan_fused`. Floor removed by a **data-driven ground-plane fit** (fits the floor line `v = A·(1/z)+B` per frame, self-calibrating camera height + pitch; sanity-bounded fallback to the assumed model). Also flags **drops/negative obstacles**: below-floor returns are flagged like positives, plus a missing-floor edge check marks the near edge of a ledge/stairs where the floor that should be in view returns nothing (Create 3 cliff sensors should cover the <0.4 m camera blind zone — pending). Only ever lowers a beam (never erases lidar); falls back to raw lidar if depth goes stale. So glass enters the **SLAM map**, not just the costmap.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:43:- `depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py` — OAK depth folded into the lidar scan → `scan_fused` (the *alternative* to depth_perception; point SLAM + Nav2 at `scan_fused`).
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:53:- **Glass handling, in layers:** depth-detected obstacles in the costmap → `glass_guard` bump backstop (persistent marks) → `depth_lidar_fusion` injects depth into the lidar scan (`scan_fused`) so glass also enters the **SLAM map** *(built + offline-validated; on-robot localization check pending)*.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:60:3. **Create 3 hazards:** `hazard_detection` is **event-only** (publishes only *during* a hazard) and only on the **raw** `/turtlebot468/_do_not_use/hazard_detection` (NOT republished to the clean namespace). Create 3 webserver: **192.168.186.2** (Pi is .3 over usb0), firmware H.2.6.
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:84:# OR: fold depth into the lidar scan -> scan_fused (so glass enters the SLAM map)
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:86:# compare in RViz: raw /turtlebot468/scan vs fused /turtlebot468/scan_fused (best_effort QoS)
/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md:94:- **Next (on robot):** point slam_toolbox `scan_topic` + Nav2 costmap source at `scan_fused` so the glass enters the **SLAM map** (not just the live topic), run a **localization sanity check** (map stays crisp, no double-walls), then a full autonomous mapping run.

content.text:
I have enough to assess this doc thoroughly. It's a pure networking/operations doc (no claims about the ROS2 package code/launch/config, so no code drift to spot-check against `src/`). Let me verify internal consistency on the few cross-doc facts that overlap.

Key cross-check observations:
- The doc's `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER` IP `10.247.204.21:11811` matches `nuwave-connection.md` and `network/README.md`.
- Root cause #2 (Create 3 stale discovery IP `192.168.50.31` → `192.168.186.3:11811`) is consistent with `network/README.md`'s cleanup note.
- The Create 3 webserver IP `192.168.186.2` matches CLAUDE.md gotcha #3.
- The `wifi.interface → wlan0` and `nuwave-connection.md` `regdom=US` (5 GHz) reference are consistent.
- OAK-D `X_LINK_ERROR` matches CLAUDE.md gotcha #8.

One minor internal tension: the laptop is described as `10.247.228.155/19` with subnet `10.247.224.0/19` and gateway `10.247.224.1`; the doc says "different /19 segments, routed." This is internally consistent and matches the sibling docs. No factual drift against actual code (the doc makes no code/launch/config claims).

input.purpose: Operational recipe + debugging trail for running full ROS 2 (laptop to robot) over Northeastern's NUwave enterprise WiFi via a FastDDS Discovery Server super-client.
input.freshness: Matches the rest of the repo's documented inventory and CLAUDE.md. All shared facts are consistent across docs: ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER IP (10.247.204.21:11811), Create 3 stale-IP fix (192.168.50.31 -> 192.168.186.3:11811, also in network/README.md cleanup note), Create 3 webserver 192.168.186.2 (CLAUDE.md gotcha #3), OAK-D X_LINK_ERROR (CLAUDE.md gotcha #8), regdom=US/5GHz (nuwave-connection.md). Makes no claims about the guide_mate_explorer/guide_mate_perception code, launch, or config, so there is no code drift to flag. Only soft-stale element is the DHCP robot/laptop IPs, which the doc itself explicitly flags as DHCP-volatile (verify on robot display). Dated June 2026, current.
input.uniqueValue: Irreplaceable: the four-root-cause debugging trail and recovery procedures that exist nowhere else. Specifically (a) the FastDDS discovery-server DATABASE-corruption failure mode where discovery works but data never flows (Subscription count: 0, laptop-published topics absent from robot graph) and its fix (systemctl restart discovery + turtlebot4) plus the journalctl health check `grep -c DISCOVERY_DATABASE Error`; (b) ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True env var works but FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE XML profile silently fails; (c) the /etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash [ -t 0 ] TTY check that forces ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=False in non-interactive scripts; (d) `ros2 topic hz` does NOT support --no-daemon (echo/list/info do); (e) measured topic rates (odom ~18Hz, scan ~7.6Hz, battery ~1Hz) and the working laptop env-var recipe; (f) verified NUwave does no harmful client isolation. The sibling docs only summarize the conclusion, not this how-to/why.
input.recommendationDetail: Keep as-is. It is the canonical laptop-to-robot ROS 2 bring-up + troubleshooting reference and the only home of the discovery-server failure modes. Some setup environment (export block, multicast-vs-Discovery-Server rationale) is restated from network/README.md, but that overlap is acceptable for a standalone runbook; if trimming is ever desired, defer the discovery-model background to network/README.md and keep the operational recipe + four-root-cause trail here. Optionally add the DHCP IP caveat inline at every IP mention (already noted once).
input.summary: A focused, high-quality network runbook: a verdict table of what works (discovery, subscribe, publish, service/param calls laptop-to-robot), the exact env-var super-client setup, measured network facts and topic rates, and a four-stage root-cause debugging trail (regdom/5GHz, Create 3 stale discovery IP, XML-profile silent failure, and discovery-database corruption). Ends with an operational checklist and copy-paste reproduce-the-tests commands. Purely networking/ops; makes no claims about the ROS package code.
qualityIssues: Robot/laptop NUwave IPs are DHCP and will drift; the doc flags this but hardcodes 10.247.204.21 in the copy-paste export block, so a stale copy-paste is likely on reconnect.
Some content (multicast-cannot-cross-subnets rationale, the env export block, Discovery Server model) is duplicated from network/README.md; acceptable for a standalone runbook but is genuine overlap.

content.text:
Audit complete. Summary of findings for `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/network/ros2-over-nuwave.md`:

- **Verdict: keep, critical.** This is the canonical laptop-to-robot ROS 2 bring-up and troubleshooting runbook, and the sole home of the FastDDS discovery-server failure modes.
- **No code drift.** The doc is pure networking/ops and makes no claims about `guide_mate_explorer`/`guide_mate_perception` code, launch, or config, so there was nothing in `src/` to contradict. All shared facts (discovery-server IP, Create 3 stale-IP fix, webserver `192.168.186.2`, OAK-D `X_LINK_ERROR`, `regdom=US`) are internally consistent with `network/README.md`, `nuwave-connection.md`, and `CLAUDE.md`.
- **Irreplaceable content:** the four-root-cause debugging trail, especially the discovery-database-corruption failure mode (discovery works but `Subscription count: 0`, no data) and its restart fix + `journalctl` health check; the `ROS_SUPER_CLIENT=True` env-var-vs-silent-XML-profile gotcha; the `/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash` `[ -t 0 ]` TTY trap; and that `ros2 topic hz` does not support `--no-daemon`.
- **Minor issues:** the export block hardcodes the DHCP robot IP `10.247.204.21` (doc warns about this but it will still drift); modest overlap with `network/README.md` on the multicast/Discovery-Server rationale, acceptable for a standalone runbook. No contradictions found.
